title: Change-Next-Match Loop — {key:cgn}
subtitle: Like {key:*}{key:c}{key:w}, but {key:.} jumps and changes in one step.
summary: After a {key:/}search, {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} changes the next match. Then {key:.} by itself jumps to the next match and changes it — no need for separate {key:n}. The pinnacle of dot-repeat refactoring.

blocks.text: {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} is the *better* version of the {key:*}{key:c}{key:w} loop. It rolls *jump-to-next* and *change* into a single dot-repeatable action.

sequence.note: Search for the thing you want to change
Jump to next match and change it
Skip a match — move to the one after

blocks.title: What {key:g}{key:n} Is
blocks.text:
{key:g}{key:n} is a *motion* that selects the next search match (forward; {key:g}{key:N} for backward). It works as both a *normal mode* motion *and* a text object — when used after an operator, it becomes the range to operate on.

So {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} = "change the next search match." The dot-repeat record is *the whole thing*: "find next match, delete it, insert `NEW`." Every {key:.} after that re-runs the entire sequence.

This is strictly better than the {key:*}{key:c}{key:w} loop because:

  • {key:.} alone advances + changes (no separate {key:n}).
  • Works when the match isn't a word (regex, multi-word, partial).
{key:d}{key:g}{key:n} for delete-next-match. {key:y}{key:g}{key:n} for yank-next-match. The motion is composable like any other.

blocks.title: {key:c}{key:g}{key:n} is the search-edit-repeat loop
blocks.text: Three subsystems cooperate: search stores the active pattern, {key:g}{key:n} resolves to a motion that selects the next match, and {key:c} consumes that motion to replace it. The replacement also updates the dot-repeat slot, so {key:.} repeats the entire change against the next match without re-issuing the search. That's the smallest non-trivial example of dot, motion, and search composition.
